# Research advances precision dairy care with AI-powered tools

## Paudyal drives innovation to support dairy farmers with data-driven tools to boost efficiency

June 25, 2025 - by [Kay Ledbetter](https://agrilifetoday.tamu.edu/author/kay-ledbetter/ "Posts by Kay Ledbetter")

As the dairy industry increasingly adopts automation with the use of sensors and robotics, researchers at [Texas A&M AgriLife](https://agrilife.tamu.edu/) are helping producers harness this evolving technology to help optimize production and improve the health and well-being of dairy cattle.

![Sushil Paudyal shows off a new invention](data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB3aWR0aD0iMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIxIiB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciPjwvc3ZnPg==)

Sushil Paudyal, Ph.D., an assistant professor of dairy science in the [Texas A&M College of Agriculture and Life Sciences](https://aglifesciences.tamu.edu/) [Department of Animal Science](https://animalscience.tamu.edu/), is helping to spearhead these efforts. He leads research that applies artificial intelligence, AI, and machine learning technology to gather advanced, real-time data on farms, developing systems that support earlier disease detection, informed decision-making and cost-effective adoption of robotics.

“Sensor-based systems, AI and real-time analytics are transforming how dairies make everyday decisions,” Paudyal said. “But to be effective, these technologies must be adaptable, updatable and tailored to individual farm needs.”

“Sensor-based systems, AI and real-time analytics are transforming how dairies make everyday decisions. But to be effective, these technologies must be adaptable, updatable and tailored to individual farm needs.”

**Sushil Paudyal, Ph.D.**  
*Assistant professor of dairy science*  
*Department of Animal Science*

### Building the future of data-driven dairy

Paudyal’s lab focuses on practical, technology-based research that helps producers stay ahead of evolving challenges, including rising heat stress and changing labor dynamics. Technology-driven models can detect diseases early, enhance cow management and improve efficiency on dairy farms. Already, he has successfully deployed models to detect lameness, mastitis and heat stress in individual dairy cows, using advanced analytics algorithms trained on camera images and behavioral cues.

“Right now, we’re developing farm efficiency models based on machine learning for robotic milking systems, aiming to pinpoint idle time and milking failures,” he said.

At the recent U.S. Precision Livestock Farming Conference in Lincoln, Nebraska, Paudyal and his team highlighted some of their research:

![a man in a cap and sunglasses and overalls, Sushil Paudyal, holds up two red lights to the teats of a dairy cow](data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB3aWR0aD0iMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIxIiB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciPjwvc3ZnPg==)
![a camera mounted on the ceiling of a livestock building to monitor the animals below](data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB3aWR0aD0iMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIxIiB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciPjwvc3ZnPg==)
![a smiling man, Sushil Paudyal, pushes a container of a gas on a dolly and holds a blue piece of equipment in his gloved hand.](data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB3aWR0aD0iMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIxIiB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciPjwvc3ZnPg==)
![an arm is holding a wand that is a tag reader and walking by black and white cattle with yellow ear tags](data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB3aWR0aD0iMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIxIiB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciPjwvc3ZnPg==)
![a rotary milking parlor](data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB3aWR0aD0iMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIxIiB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciPjwvc3ZnPg==)

### Innovation designed for real-world use

One of Paudyal’s goals is to create noninvasive, cost-effective diagnostic tools that work across diverse production systems. For example, some rely on camera-based systems in place of physical sensors to monitor large groups of cows, lowering start-up costs while expanding impact.

“We are developing sensors in our lab that can help detect diseases without collecting invasive blood samples or milk samples,” Paudyal said. “They will monitor behavior and physiological variables to determine sick cows.”

His team is currently developing a “DairyBot” virtual assistant, a generative AI tool that will enable producers to evaluate farm data and lab results, as well as ask questions about feed decisions while using AI to interpret herd data in real-time.

“They will have a real-time adviser with a vast domain of knowledge that can pull from their farm’s data and dairy-specific literature,” Paudyal said. “It won’t replace the vet or nutritionist, but it will empower and support them for informed decision-making.”

### Explore more research in animal science

Discover how the Department of Animal Science is advancing sustainable agriculture through science-based innovation.

Paudyal will present early findings at the [American Dairy Science Association](https://www.adsa.org/Meetings/2025-Annual-Meeting) conference in Louisville, Kentucky, June 22-25. A working prototype of DairyBot is expected within six months.

### Right-sized technology for all dairies

Although Paudyal said technology and real-time decision-making are the future of dairies, he emphasizes the importance of flexible, right-sized solutions. However, while many farmers see a return on investment, adoption rates vary.

He believes the camera-based systems, which monitor larger groups of cows, can reduce the upfront cost and increase adoption, ultimately helping to minimize the digital divide.

“I always want to develop solutions to the real-world problems that help dairy farmers,” Paudyal said. “As a land-grant university with a mission to support Texas dairy farmers, it is essential to develop research projects that provide practical, immediately applicable solutions. By equipping farmers with the tools and resources they need, we can help address real-world challenges on the farm more effectively.”

# Revolutionizing Livestock Farming with AI Technology

## Revolutionizing Livestock Farming with AI Technology to boost productivity, animal health, and sustainability.

![Artificial Intelligence +](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fill:64:64/1*2-mjLxHoLybNvlrURa0TIQ.png)

--

Share

![Revolutionizing Livestock Farming with AI Technology]()

## **Revolutionizing Livestock Farming with AI Technology**

**Revolutionizing Livestock Farming with AI Technology** starts with understanding how digital innovation is reshaping traditional agriculture. AI-powered tools are creating real-time solutions for farmers who need to manage livestock more efficiently. From monitoring animal health to improving breeding practices, artificial intelligence is emerging as the key to solving the most complex challenges in the livestock sector. If you’re a farmer aiming to increase productivity, an investor eyeing agri-tech markets, or a policymaker focused on sustainability, this article is for you. Dive into the future of smart livestock farming and discover how AI is setting new standards in animal agriculture.

***Also Read:*** [***AI in Livestock Management***](https://www.aiplusinfo.com/blog/ai-in-livestock-management/)

enriched_points.point_title: Advancements in AI and machine learning
enriched_points.expanded_content: AI and machine learning are revolutionizing livestock farming by enabling real-time monitoring of animal health, optimizing feeding patterns, and automating management processes to boost productivity.

Blockchain technology enhances traceability in livestock supply chains, ensuring transparency in animal welfare practices, feed origins, and disease management, fostering consumer trust and regulatory compliance.

Innovative technologies, including drones and IoT devices, are being deployed for precision livestock farming, improving disease detection, resource management, and environmental sustainability through data-driven insights.
